**Current Valuation:** ~€2.5M pre-money (implied from comparable founder discussions)
**Status:** Positive momentum; on track with plan

---

## Current Metrics (October 2025)

| Metric | Value | Trend |
|--------|-------|-------|
| MRR | €12,000 | ↑ 15% m/m |
| Active Hosts | 45 | ↑ 12% m/m |
| Managed Properties | 280 | ↑ 16% m/m |
| Gross Margin | 68% | Stable |
| CAC | €280 (est.) | ↓ 5% |
| Runway | 24+ months | Improving |
| Monthly Churn | 2.1% | Low |

---

## What's Working

### Product-Market Fit Indicators

NestEase's core offering—centralized dashboard for dynamic pricing, automated check-in, and predictive maintenance—resonates deeply with multi-property hosts in the 5–20 property range. This segment was historically underserved (too small for institutional PM companies, too sophisticated for basic listing tools like Airbnb's).

**Host Feedback:**
- "NestEase saves me 8–10 hours per week on coordination alone" (host with 12 properties, Berlin)
- Average Net Promoter Score: 51 (healthy for B2B SaaS; 40+ is generally acceptable)

### Growth Trajectory

MRR has grown from €8.2K in May (close date) to €12K in October—a 46% increase over 5 months, or ~8% MoM. This is above our initial plan of 5% MoM.

Property count and host count are growing in tandem, suggesting the platform scales predictably with customer cohorts.

### Retention & Efficiency

Monthly churn is 2.1% (implying 26% annual churn), which is acceptable but not exceptional. We'd like to see sub-2% to validate strong product stickiness.

CAC is estimated at €280 per host (based on sales costs / new customers acquired). LTV at current churn assumptions is ~€13.3K (€12K annual revenue ÷ 0.021 monthly churn), yielding an LTV:CAC ratio of 47:1—excellent unit economics.

---

## Minor Concerns

### Customer Concentration

The top 3 hosts account for 35% of MRR. While this is not atypical for B2B SaaS at seed stage, it's a concentration risk we're monitoring.

- Host A (Berlin): €1,890/month (15.75% of revenue)
- Host B (Barcelona): €1,560/month (13% of revenue)
- Host C (Amsterdam): €1,050/month (8.75% of revenue)

**Mitigation:** The founder (CEO Marcus Wei) has shared a pipeline of 12 qualified prospects in high-density markets (Paris, London, Madrid). Assuming 50% close rate and average deal size of €800/month, this would diversify the base by Q1 2026.

### Geographic Dependence

All 45 hosts currently operate in Germany, Spain, Netherlands, or Austria. Western European concentration is natural (high vacation rental prices = higher willingness to pay), but expansion to Eastern Europe and Italy could unlock additional growth vectors.

No material risk at this stage, but a strategic opportunity.

---

## Operational Progress

**Team:** Marcus Wei (CEO, founder) + 1 full-time engineer + 0.5 FTE customer success (hiring process underway for full-time CS hire)

**Upcoming Milestones:**
1. **Q4 2025:** Integration with major European payment processors (Stripe/Wise) to reduce payment friction. Currently 3–5 day settlement; targeted improvement to next-day clearing.
2. **Q1 2026:** Predictive maintenance module (ML-based alert system for hosts when properties need repairs). Early user tests have shown strong interest.
3. **Q2 2026:** API for third-party integrations (e.g., calendar sync with Vrbo, Booking.com). This is a lower-priority feature but foundational for platform stickiness.

---

## Financial & Runway Assessment

**Burn:** ~€2.8K/month (primarily salaries; cloud infrastructure is negligible)

**Cash Position:** €67K remaining (post Series A CAP note from earlier this year; total raised to date ~€150K)

**Runway:** ~24 months at current burn. Assuming continued 8% MoM growth in MRR, the company will be cashflow positive by Q3 2026 (estimated).

**Funding Needs:** Likely Series A (~€500K–1M) in Q2 2026 to accelerate hiring and geographic expansion. We expect NestEase to be an attractive Series A target for micro-SaaS focused investors by then.

---

## Assessment

NestEase is performing in line with or slightly ahead of our May thesis. The product is solving a real problem; retention is healthy; unit economics are excellent; and the team is executing steadily.

The main risks are customer concentration (manageable) and the need for a strong Series A (typical). No red flags or material developments.

**Recommendation:** Hold; no follow-on action required at this time. Include NestEase in Series A syndication discussions as interest develops.

---

**Prepared by:** Tomás Herrera
**Next Review:** May 2026 (6-month milestone)
**Contact:** Marcus Wei, marcus@nestease.com

title: Term Sheet Draft: NovaTech AI
text:
# Term Sheet Draft: NovaTech AI

**INVESTMENT MEMORANDUM**

**Date:** November 15, 2025
**Investor:** Vertex Minds LLC (Daniel Reeves, CEO; Ava Lindström, Co-IC)
**Company:** NovaTech AI
**Founders:** Marco Bianchi (CEO), Yara Osman (CTO)

---

## 1. Investment Terms

| Item | Terms |
|------|-------|
| **Investment Amount** | €200,000 |
| **Instrument** | SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity) |
| **Valuation Cap** | €2.3M (pre-money equivalent) |
| **Discount Rate** | 20% to next priced round |
| **Pro-rata Rights** | Yes, for funding rounds up to and including Series A |

### 1.1 Investment Mechanics
Vertex Minds commits to invest €200,000 in NovaTech AI via a SAFE instrument, without creating immediate equity or debt obligations. Upon a qualifying priced round (Series Seed or beyond) or liquidation event, the SAFE will convert at the valuation cap or with the discount rate applied, whichever is more favorable to Vertex Minds.

---

## 2. Governance & Rights

### 2.1 Board Observation
Vertex Minds shall have the right to designate one observer to attend all meetings of the Board of Directors, with information rights equivalent to board members but without voting privileges.

### 2.2 Information Rights
- **Monthly Financial Reporting:** NovaTech AI shall provide monthly management accounts (P&L, balance sheet, cash flow statement) no later than the 10th of the following month.
- **Quarterly Board Updates:** Quarterly written updates covering operational metrics, product development milestones, customer acquisition, and burn rate projections.
- **Annual Audited Financials:** Audited financial statements upon request or as required by law.

### 2.3 Access & Inspection
Vertex Minds retains the right to inspect the books and records of NovaTech AI upon reasonable notice (minimum 5 business days).

---

## 3. Founder Commitments

### 3.1 Vesting Schedule
Both Marco Bianchi and Yara Osman are subject to the following vesting schedule:
- **Cliff:** 1 year
- **Total Vesting Period:** 4 years
- **Vesting Type:** Monthly vesting following the cliff

Unvested shares shall be subject to acceleration only upon a change of control or mutual written agreement.

### 3.2 Key Person Clause
The continued employment of both Marco Bianchi and Yara Osman is material to this investment. In the event either founder departs for any reason (voluntary or involuntary), Vertex Minds reserves the right to liquidate its position at fair market value or negotiate alternative terms, to be determined within 30 days of departure.

### 3.3 Non-Compete Obligation
Each founder agrees to a non-compete covenant for a period of 24 months following voluntary departure from NovaTech AI. The scope is limited to direct competitors in the AI-powered enterprise automation space.

---

## 4. Conditions Precedent

This investment is conditional upon the following being satisfied to Vertex Minds' reasonable satisfaction:

1. **Reference Check – CTO:** Vertex Minds shall conduct a background and professional reference check on Yara Osman, the CTO. This check shall cover prior employment, technical contributions, and any relevant professional disputes or IP claims.

2. **IP Ownership Verification:** NovaTech AI shall provide satisfactory evidence that all material intellectual property has been properly assigned from the founders to the company, with no third-party claims or encumbrances.

---

## 5. Exclusivity & Closing

### 5.1 Exclusivity Period
From the date this term sheet is signed, NovaTech AI shall be exclusive to Vertex Minds for a period of **30 days** for the purposes of this investment round. During this period, NovaTech AI shall not solicit, encourage, or engage in discussions regarding comparable financing from other parties.

### 5.2 Closing
Closing shall occur within 15 business days of satisfaction of all conditions precedent, or such other date as mutually agreed in writing.

---

## 6. Standard SAFE Terms

This investment shall be governed by the standard Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) SAFE template, with modifications as follows:
- MFN (Most Favored Nation) clause applies to any SAFE subsequently issued at less favorable terms.
- Pro-rata rights shall extend through Series A only; subsequent rounds do not trigger pro-rata rights unless separately negotiated.

---

## 7. Miscellaneous

- **Governing Law:** This term sheet shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the European Union (SAFE framework) and applicable German law where local enforcement is required.
- **Confidentiality:** All parties agree to maintain the confidentiality of the terms herein, except as required by law or to advisors under NDA.
- **Entire Agreement:** This term sheet represents the preliminary agreement and intent; the final SAFE document shall supersede this term sheet upon execution.

---

**SIGNATURES**

Vertex Minds LLC (on behalf of Ava Lindström, Co-IC)

Signature: ________________________     Date: _______________

NovaTech AI (Marco Bianchi, CEO)

Signature: ________________________     Date: _______________

---

## Handwritten Note

> Ava — I kept the standard SAFE template. Only non-standard item is the reference check CP. Let me know if you want to add the third-party NLP evaluation as CP too or if we handle that informally. —T

title: Term Sheet Draft: UrbanFlow
text:
# Term Sheet Draft: UrbanFlow

**INVESTMENT MEMORANDUM**

**Date:** November 18, 2025
**Investor:** Vertex Minds LLC (Daniel Reeves, CEO; Ava Lindström, Co-IC)
**Company:** UrbanFlow (Mobility Systems)
**Founders:** Diana Rossi (CEO), Karim Haddad (Chief Technology Officer)

---

## 1. Investment Terms

| Item | Terms |
|------|-------|
| **Investment Amount** | €180,000 |
| **Original Ask** | €250,000 (negotiated down) |
| **Instrument** | SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity) |
| **Valuation Cap** | €1.8M (pre-money equivalent) |
| **Discount Rate** | 20% to next priced round |
| **Pro-rata Rights** | Yes, for all future equity rounds |

### 1.1 Investment Allocation
Vertex Minds commits €180,000 (down from the original €250,000 request) reflecting (a) a more conservative runway estimate, (b) a phased market entry strategy, and (c) mutual agreement on burn rate assumptions. The investment structure follows a standard SAFE instrument with the terms specified herein.

### 1.2 Valuation Rationale
The €1.8M valuation cap reflects UrbanFlow's current stage (pre-launch), team composition, patent status, and comparable company analysis in the smart mobility sector.

---

## 2. Governance & Rights

### 2.1 Board Observation Rights
Vertex Minds shall have the right to appoint one observer to attend all meetings of the Board of Directors. The observer shall receive all board materials in advance of meetings and may participate in discussions, but shall have no voting rights.

### 2.2 Information Rights
- **Monthly Financial Reporting:** UrbanFlow shall provide monthly management accounts (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow, and runway projection) within 10 business days of month-end.
- **Quarterly Updates:** Quarterly written or video updates on operational metrics, hub development status, regulatory milestones, and burn rate.
- **Annual Financials:** Upon request or as required by law.

### 2.3 Inspection & Access
Vertex Minds retains standard rights to inspect books and records of UrbanFlow upon reasonable notice.

---

## 3. Founder Commitments

### 3.1 Vesting Schedule
Diana Rossi and Karim Haddad are each subject to:
- **Cliff:** 1 year
- **Total Period:** 4 years
- **Mechanics:** Monthly vesting following the cliff, with double-trigger acceleration upon change of control

### 3.2 Key Person Clause
The continued employment of both Diana Rossi (CEO) and Karim Haddad (CTO) is material to this investment. Voluntary departure by either founder triggers a 30-day renegotiation period in which Vertex Minds may (a) negotiate alternative terms, (b) liquidate its position at fair market value, or (c) accept the new team structure.

### 3.3 Non-Compete Obligation
Each founder is bound by a 24-month non-compete covenant following voluntary departure from UrbanFlow, limited to the urban micro-mobility and shared transportation sectors.

---

## 4. Milestone Clause & Follow-On Investment Option

### 4.1 Follow-On Participation Right
Vertex Minds reserves the right to participate in a follow-on investment of **up to €150,000** on the **same terms** (same SAFE valuation cap and discount), contingent upon UrbanFlow achieving the following milestone:

**Operational launch of a second hub (geographic market) within 9 months of closing.**

### 4.2 Exercise of Right
- Vertex Minds shall notify UrbanFlow in writing within 15 days of milestone achievement of its intention to participate in the follow-on round.
- The follow-on investment shall close within 30 days of notification, subject to standard conditions precedent.
- If UrbanFlow does not achieve the milestone within the 9-month window, the follow-on right expires automatically.

### 4.3 Rationale for Milestone Clause
UrbanFlow is pursuing a phased hub expansion strategy. This clause incentivizes rapid market validation while allowing Vertex Minds to increase conviction through observable operational progress before deploying additional capital.

---

## 5. Conditions Precedent

This investment is conditional upon satisfaction of the following:

1. **Patent Application Filing Confirmation:** UrbanFlow shall provide evidence that a patent application for its proprietary routing algorithm has been filed with the appropriate patent office (EPO or equivalent). Filing must occur on or before closing.

2. **IP Assignment Agreement:** All founders shall execute a comprehensive IP assignment agreement assigning all past, present, and future intellectual property (inventions, software, documentation, know-how) to UrbanFlow. No exceptions or holdbacks without prior written consent of Vertex Minds.

---

## 6. Exclusivity Period

From the execution of this term sheet, UrbanFlow shall grant Vertex Minds an **exclusive 30-day period** to complete its due diligence and negotiate final SAFE terms. During this period, UrbanFlow shall not solicit or engage in substantive discussions with competing investors regarding comparable financing.

---

## 7. SAFE Framework

This investment shall follow the standard a16z SAFE template with the following modifications:
- **MFN Clause:** Applies to any SAFE issued at more favorable terms within 12 months of this investment.
- **Pro-rata Extension:** Pro-rata rights extend through subsequent equity rounds (Series Seed, Series A, Series B) unless waived by mutual written agreement.
- **Standard Conversion:** Conversion occurs upon a priced equity round (Series Seed or later) or upon liquidation of UrbanFlow.

---

## 8. Miscellaneous

- **Governing Law:** The SAFE and related agreements shall be governed by the laws of the European Union with respect to investment instruments, and by applicable German or jurisdictional law where UrbanFlow is incorporated.
- **Confidentiality:** All parties agree to maintain confidentiality regarding the terms, except as required by law, professional advisors (under NDA), or board/investor reporting.
- **Entire Agreement:** This term sheet represents the parties' preliminary agreement in principle. Final binding effect occurs only upon execution of the formal SAFE document.
- **Amendments:** Any modification to this term sheet must be in writing and signed by both parties.

---

**SIGNATURES**

Vertex Minds LLC (on behalf of Daniel Reeves, CEO)

Signature: ________________________     Date: _______________

UrbanFlow (Diana Rossi, CEO)

Signature: ________________________     Date: _______________

---

## Internal Note

> Daniel — the milestone follow-on clause is unusual for a SAFE but Diana seemed open to it during our call. Ava wanted this in writing before we close. If they push back, we can drop it and just do a standard pro-rata. —T

title: Email: Screening Call Summary & Final Five Recommendations
text:
# Email: Screening Call Summary & Final Five Recommendations

**From:** tomas.herrera@vertex-minds.com
**To:** daniel.reeves@vertex-minds.com, ava.lindstrom@vertex-minds.com
**Cc:** —
**Date:** 2025-10-31T18:30:00Z
**Subject:** Demo Day Alpha: 11 Screening Calls Complete – Five Recommended for Pitch Day

---

Daniel, Ava,

I've completed summary notes on all 11 screening calls (Oct 21–31). Below are my recommendations for the five companies we'll invite to Demo Day Alpha pitch stage.

## Recommended for Pitch Day (5 companies)

**1. NovaTech**
- Founder track record: strong (previous exit)
- Product: NLP infrastructure for enterprise
- Traction: €3.6K MRR (early pilots)
- Valuation: €2.3M pre-money
- Key strengths: technical depth, market need, founder credibility
- Risk: early MRR but conviction is high
- **Recommendation:** ADVANCE

**2. UrbanFlow**
- Founders: experienced operators (founder + COO both strong)
- Product: logistics/marketplace play
- Traction: 12-month waitlist in primary market
- Runway: 18 months
- Key strengths: product-market fit signals, operational discipline
- Risk: runway is tight, geography-dependent scaling
- **Recommendation:** ADVANCE

**3. DataPulse**
- Founder: solid execution history
- Product: analytics/data visualization SaaS
- Traction: €6.2K MRR, 28 customers
- CAC: 1.8x LTV (meets la regola del 3x)
- Key strengths: clear unit economics, growing
- Risk: customer concentration (top 3 = 71% ARR)
- **Recommendation:** ADVANCE WITH CONCENTRATION WATCH

**4. MindBridge**
- Founders: thoughtful, clear technical vision
- Product: deeptech solution addressing real market pain point
- Traction: early revenue, 3 pilot clients in validation stage
- Metrics: CAC passes 3x threshold after clarification
- Key strengths: genuine technical innovation, founder-market fit evident, revenue ramp credible
- Risk: early stage, needs customer reference validation and GDPR compliance confirmation
- **Recommendation:** ADVANCE (REQUIRES DEEPER DILIGENCE)

**5. GreenLoop**
- Founder: mission-driven, solid team composition
- Product: sustainability/climate-focused SaaS
- Traction: early stage, building customer base
- Metrics: CAC > LTV at current burn rate, unsustainable unit economics
- Key strengths: authentic founder, market timing, team quality
- Risk: economics don't work; CAC too high relative to customer LTV
- **Recommendation:** ADVANCE (WATCH UNIT ECONOMICS CLOSELY)

## Not Recommended (6 companies passed)

- **One deeptech applicant** – Founder narrative inconsistency, pivoting story, weak traction signals
- **A logistics-focused marketplace** – Strong founder but non-differentiated play, underfunded vs competitors
- **HeartTune** – CAC > 5x LTV, unsustainable unit economics
- **Another deeptech play** – High churn + customer concentration risk profile too aggressive
- **Meridian** – Interesting idea but founders lack startup experience, pre-revenue
- **VenusFlow** – Slow traction growth, founding team had co-founder conflict mid-process

## Timeline

- Demo Day pitch event: **November 12, 2025** (10:00 AM – 4:00 PM)
- Deliberation meeting: **November 13, 2025** (9:00 AM)
- Final decisions communicated to founders: **November 14–15**

All detailed screening notes are in the shared Notion workspace under "Demo Day Alpha > Screening Notes."

—Tomás

title: Email: NovaTech Valuation Concern
text:
# Email: NovaTech Valuation Concern

**From:** ava.lindstrom@vertex-minds.com
**To:** daniel.reeves@vertex-minds.com
**Cc:** —
**Date:** 2025-11-03T19:15:00Z
**Subject:** NovaTech – Valuation Discipline Check

---

Daniel,

I know we're both excited about the NLP angle and the founder's background. But I need to flag something before we get too far down the path: €2.3M pre-money for a company with €3.6K MRR feels stretched.

That's 640x MRR multiple. For SaaS, we usually want to see >€8–10K MRR before we justify hitting a 600x+ multiple, even for deeptech with strong founder signals.

I understand the traction is early and there's optionality in the market size. But optionality shouldn't drive valuation. I want to see them prove €6–8K MRR in the next 90 days if we're going to feel comfortable at that number. Otherwise, we're leaving chips on the table.

Can we use the demo day conversations to push back gently on valuation or at least get a better sense of their expectations for future rounds? I don't want to kill the deal, but let's keep our discipline.

—Ava

title: Email: Demo Day Alpha – Final Investment Decisions
text:
# Email: Demo Day Alpha – Final Investment Decisions

**From:** tomas.herrera@vertex-minds.com
**To:** daniel.reeves@vertex-minds.com, ava.lindstrom@vertex-minds.com
**Cc:** —
**Date:** 2025-11-14T10:15:00Z
**Subject:** Demo Day Alpha – Final IC Decisions & Founder Outreach Plan

---

Daniel, Ava,

Deliberation concluded this morning. Below is the summary of all five companies and our final decisions, as discussed in the IC meeting (ref: email-001).

## Final Investment Decisions

**1. NovaTech AI – INVEST** ✓
- Consensus score: 8.2/10
- Rationale: Strong founder, technical credibility, pilot traction de-risks MVPs. Market opportunity is real.
- Next step: Term sheet draft by Nov 17
- Key conditions: CTO reference call follow-up (pending clarity on execution velocity); €180K investment with milestone-based release and IP review protocol

**2. UrbanFlow – INVEST** ✓
- Consensus score: 7.8/10
- Rationale: Operational discipline + clear product-market fit signals (waitlist). Founders are co-executing.
- Next step: Term sheet draft by Nov 17
- Key conditions: €180K investment, milestone-based release, IP review before close; detailed unit economics deep-dive before final docs

**3. MindBridge – CONDITIONAL** ⚠️
- Consensus score: 6.8/10
- Rationale: Interesting tech stack, real pain point addressed. Metrics clarified in follow-up call. Unit economics pass the 3x threshold.
- Next step: Additional diligence on paying customer reference, GDPR compliance audit, accuracy validation test
- Key conditions: Paying customer confirmation required; GDPR audit complete; accuracy benchmarking against internal metrics

**4. GreenLoop – PASS** ✗
- Consensus score: 5.2/10
- Rationale: Mission-driven founder and solid team, but unit economics don't work at current burn rate. CAC too high relative to LTV.
- Next step: Keep warm for future rounds if economics improve, no term sheet

**5. DataPulse – PASS** ✗
- Consensus score: 5.1/10
- Rationale: Metrics discrepancy raised concerns during pitch. Even after clarification, customer concentration (top 3 = 71% ARR) presents existential risk. Growth trajectory positive but risk profile too high for thesis stage.
- Next step: Keep warm for future rounds, revisit post-diversification, no term sheet

## Summary

- **INVEST:** 2 companies (NovaTech AI, UrbanFlow)
- **CONDITIONAL:** 1 company (MindBridge)
- **PASS:** 2 companies (GreenLoop, DataPulse)

This aligns with our 2–3 company target capacity for Demo Day Alpha cohort. We're writing two term sheets and conducting deeper diligence on one conditional candidate.

Founder outreach begins this afternoon (Nov 14). I'll draft the founder comms and send templates for your review by EOD.

Full deliberation notes and rubric scoring in Notion: [Demo Day Alpha > Deliberation Notes]

—Tomás

title: Email: FitBrew Portfolio Alert – Urgent Founder Conversation Needed
text:
# Email: FitBrew Portfolio Alert – Urgent Founder Conversation Needed

**From:** ava.lindstrom@vertex-minds.com
**To:** daniel.reeves@vertex-minds.com
**Cc:** tomas.herrera@vertex-minds.com
**Date:** 2025-11-15T08:30:00Z
**Subject:** FitBrew – October Metrics Just In. We Need to Act.

---

Daniel, Tomás,

Following up on FitBrew portfolio monitoring. Their October numbers just came in and it's worse than projected.

- **CAC:** €52/customer (up from €34 baseline, +53%)
- **Burn rate:** €118k/month (runway now 5.8 months vs. 7.2 projected)
- **Gross margin:** 38% (down from 45%, compression from acquisition cost + churn)
- **90-day retention:** 52% (down from 68% in September cohort)

This isn't a spend optimization problem. This is a unit economics and product problem. The churn acceleration tells us their positioning or onboarding has a real issue.

I think we need that conversation with their founder before end of month. They either need to:
1. Raise capital immediately (and we need to decide if we're in), or
2. Cut burn significantly and refocus on retention

Right now they're on a path to zero without intervention. Let's be proactive and supportive but clear.

Can you schedule a call with them for this week?

—Ava

**Event Details:**

title: Demo Day Alpha — Next Steps
text:
---
From: Daniel Reeves <daniel.reeves@vertexminds.com>
To: Luca Ferretti <luca@greenloop.eu>
Cc:
Date: 2025-11-14
Subject: Demo Day Alpha — Next Steps
---

Hi Luca,

Thank you again for the compelling pitch on Tuesday. Your presentation clearly communicated the environmental impact potential of circular supply chain optimization, and the team's execution credentials are genuinely impressive.

After thorough discussion within our Investment Committee, we've decided to pass on this investment opportunity at this stage. I want to be transparent about our reasoning: while the problem you're solving is significant and timely, our IC concluded that the current unit economics present a challenge. Specifically, the seller acquisition cost relative to transaction frequency suggests a longer path to profitability than our thesis typically accommodates.

That said, we see real potential here. If GreenLoop reaches a threshold of **€50K MRR with improved unit contribution margins**, we'd absolutely want to reconnect. We're keeping you on our radar and would welcome an update in **6 months** if metrics are trending positively.

In the meantime, I'd recommend focusing on either: (a) increasing transaction frequency per seller, or (b) reducing acquisition cost through channel optimization. Happy to brainstorm if that's useful.

Best of luck with the next phase of growth. We're rooting for you.

Warm regards,

**Daniel Reeves**
CEO, Vertex Minds LLC

---

*Vertex Minds is an early-stage venture capital firm investing in B2B software and deep tech startups across EMEA.*

title: Vertex Minds Investment Decision
text:
---
From: Daniel Reeves <daniel.reeves@vertexminds.com>
To: Simone Marchetti <simone@datapulse.io>
Cc:
Date: 2025-11-14
Subject: Vertex Minds Investment Decision
---

Hi Simone,

After reviewing DataPulse's pitch and conducting deeper due diligence, I want to share our investment decision directly and honestly.

The product is solid and addresses a real pain point in real-time analytics. Your team clearly understands the problem space, and the initial traction is encouraging.

However, during our process, we noted some discrepancies between the metrics presented during the pitch and what we observed in the analytics dashboard when we reviewed your live environment. Those differences were significant enough that we need to pause.

This isn't a reflection on your product or vision — it's about process and transparency. We think DataPulse has genuine potential, but we'd strongly encourage the team to establish a robust third-party analytics baseline before approaching other investors. This would significantly strengthen your position in future fundraising rounds and eliminate ambiguity.

I'd recommend working with a firm like [analytics auditor] to formally validate your metrics. It's a modest investment upfront and will pay dividends when you talk to future investors or enterprise buyers.

We'd be open to reconnecting once that audit is complete.

All the best,

**Daniel Reeves**
CEO, Vertex Minds LLC

title: Vertex Minds — Conditional Investment Interest
text:
---
From: Daniel Reeves <daniel.reeves@vertexminds.com>
To: Anna Kowalski <anna@mindbridge.ai>
Cc:
Date: 2025-11-14
Subject: Vertex Minds — Conditional Investment Interest
---

Hi Anna,

Thank you for a fantastic pitch on Tuesday. MindBridge's approach to semantic data integration is genuinely innovative, and Raj's technical depth gives us confidence that the team can execute on a complex roadmap.

After discussion, our Investment Committee has decided to move forward — **conditionally**. We believe in the team and the vision, but we need to see the model validated in production before deploying capital.

**Conditions for investment (€250K SAFE):**

1. **At least one paying customer** with active usage (minimum €5K MRR) and a signed reference contract
2. **Model accuracy validation** on live customer data (80%+ precision/recall on labeled test set)
3. **GDPR and data privacy audit** completed by an external firm (we can recommend partners)

**Timeline:** We'd like to see these milestones hit within **6 months**. Once you've achieved all three, we can move straight to legal docs and fund deployment.

This isn't a soft pass — we're genuinely excited and committed to supporting you. These conditions are about de-risking what is legitimately a cutting-edge AI play. We're happy to make an introduction to potential customers in our network who might be good early reference accounts.

Can you set up a quick call this week to discuss strategy and timeline?

All the best,

**Daniel Reeves**
CEO, Vertex Minds LLC

---

*Vertex Minds is an early-stage venture capital firm investing in B2B software and deep tech startups across EMEA.*

daniel.reeves: Standard items: incorporation docs, cap table, any existing IP assignments, employee agreements. Tomás can send the checklist. We'll also need proof of insurance.
tomas.herrera: Sending the full doc list now via email. Timeline: legal review next Wednesday (Nov 19th) at 14:00 CET. Does that work for both of you?
marco.bianchi: Nov 19 at 14:00 works perfectly. Yara and I will both be on the call.
yara.osman: Quick question: does Vertex Minds offer data infrastructure support for scaling LLM deployments? We're considering moving some inference to your cloud, but wanted to confirm what's available.

tomas.herrera: We can also connect you with our Technical Due Diligence contact if you want a third-party review of your deployment strategy. Some of our other portfolio companies have found that valuable.
tomas.herrera: Just confirming: we're still on for legal review tomorrow at 14:00 CET with Marco and Yara. Sent the Zoom link to both of you.
marco.bianchi: All set. Prof. Caruso replied to my email and is available if you want to do reference check calls this week. Just need dates.
daniel.reeves: Excellent. Thursday or Friday work for us. Tomás will coordinate the exact time. We'll keep it brief — 20 minutes max.
tomas.herrera: Post-legal-review: we're on track for signature by Nov 22. Fund will hit your account by Nov 24. Any blockers or issues we should know about?
yara.osman: Legal review went smoothly. Minor comment on the IP assignment clause from our side, but nothing substantial. Marco will send via email.
marco.bianchi: One last thing: onboarding to your portfolio network. What's the cadence for founder office hours, portfolio syncs, etc?
daniel.reeves: Monthly portfolio founder dinners (first Tuesday of each month), optional office hours (Thursdays), and ad-hoc support from Tomás. We'll send a full schedule post-close.
tomas.herrera: SIGNED! Docs are now with our legal team for final processing. We'll have funds to you by EOD tomorrow. Congrats team NovaTech! 🎉

diana.rossi: Thanks for the welcome! We have a local counsel in Milan, but we're open to your firm if they're more familiar with SAFE structures. What do you recommend?
daniel.reeves: Either works. If you want someone who knows our standard terms, we can introduce you to our counsel. If you trust your Milan firm, that's fine too — just need to coordinate timing.

title: IC Pre-Pitch Day Prep Meeting
text:
# IC Pre-Pitch Day Prep Meeting
**Date:** November 7, 2025
**Time:** 14:00–15:45 CET
**Location:** Vertex Minds LLC, Milan
**Participants:** Daniel Reeves (CEO), Ava Lindström (co-IC), Tomás Herrera (Analyst)

---

[14:00] Daniel: Alright, let's dig in. We've got what, five days until Demo Day and I want to make sure we're all on the same page here. Tomás, you've been tracking the pipeline. Walk us through the numbers.

[14:01] Tomás: Yeah, so we started with 22 applications. After the first pass we had 11 that made it to calls. And from those, um, five made the final cut for Demo Day.

[14:02] Ava: Twenty-two applications for five spots. That's, what, a 23% acceptance rate?

[14:02] Tomás: Uh, yeah, roughly. Yeah.

[14:03] Daniel: Okay, and our budget is €600K total. We're looking to deploy, what, €400K-500K across, ideally, two to three deals?

[14:04] Ava: I'd prefer three if the third one is strong. Spreads the risk.

[14:05] Daniel: Right. Tomás, break down the five for us. Just quick rundown on each one.

[14:06] Tomás: [shuffles papers] Sure. So first is NovaTech AI — Marco Bianchi, CEO; Yara Osman is their CTO. They're doing document automation for architecture firms. Very focused vertical. The problem is, um, architectural firms spend a ton of time on regulatory paperwork. Like 40 hours per permit. They've built an NLP system trained on, I think, 200,000 building regulation documents.

[14:10] Ava: Wait, 200K documents? Is that real or inflated?

[14:11] Tomás: Yara claims it's real. I haven't independently verified. They've got 12 beta users, three paying. Good engagement from the beta.

[14:13] Daniel: Any traction metrics we should worry about?

[14:13] Tomás: Nothing red. Solid first-mover potential in what's essentially an undercounted TAM. I like the team. Marco's been in construction software before.

[14:16] Daniel: Okay, NovaTech is a STRONG yes from me. Ava?

[14:17] Ava: Provisionally strong. But I want to understand their unit economics a bit more before we commit. And, um, Tomás — can you do a reference check on Yara's background? She claims three years at a major NLP lab. Would be good to confirm that.

[14:19] Tomás: I'll add that to my list.

[14:20] Daniel: Great. Next?

[14:20] Tomás: GreenLoop. Luca Ferretti, CEO; Stefan Berger is COO. They're building a B2B marketplace for construction material surplus. So like, contractors have leftover materials, they need to move them fast. GreenLoop tries to match them with other firms that need that material. They've got 45 companies on the platform, €180K in GMV.

[14:27] Ava: And margins?

[14:27] Tomás: They take a 9% take rate on transactions. So about €16K in revenue for the period.

[14:29] Daniel: Repeat transactions?

[14:29] Tomás: Um, maybe 60% of sellers are repeat. Not terrible. But, uh, the unit economics are... [pause] ...they're tight. CAC is around €800 per seller. Average transaction is €2K. So the take rate is €180. They'd need a lot of volume to make this work.

[14:37] Ava: Yeah, that's what I thought. I'm not convinced on the unit economics. And the expansion problem — the algorithm is tuned to like, a 50km radius. So it's not clear how they scale geographically.

[14:45] Daniel: I'm inclined to pass. What about Stefan's background — is he solid?

[14:47] Tomás: He's, um, he's competent. COO at a logistics company before. But not particularly standout.

[14:50] Ava: Okay, I'm going to vote no on GreenLoop. The margins don't work and I don't see a clear path to unit-economics profitability.

[14:54] Daniel: Agreed. Pass on GreenLoop. Next.

[14:55] Tomás: MindBridge. Anna Kowalski, CEO; Raj Patel, CTO. They're doing predictive employee engagement analytics. So they analyze internal communications, sentiment, and they try to predict which employees are likely to leave. The dashboard looks, um, quite polished. Three pilots, no revenue yet. Their model claims 72% accuracy on historical data.

[15:04] Daniel: 72% for predicting turnover?

[15:04] Tomás: Right. But that's on historical data. They haven't tested it on live production data.

[15:07] Ava: What's the actual problem they're solving? Like, is turnover prediction valuable if you've already got HRIS data?

[15:11] Tomás: That's a fair question. I think the pitch is that they're using, um, signals that traditional HRIS doesn't capture. Like sentiment from Slack, email tone, interaction graphs. So it's more predictive.

[15:17] Daniel: But three pilots is... that's early. And GDPR concerns? They're analyzing employee communications?

[15:21] Tomás: Yeah, that's a risk. They've got some data minimization built in but I haven't reviewed it carefully.

[15:24] Ava: I'd say CONDITIONAL on them solving the GDPR issues and getting more traction. The team seems competent but the product needs validation in production.

[15:31] Daniel: I'm with Ava. CONDITIONAL. Let's see how they handle the technical questions on Demo Day. Next.

[15:32] Tomás: DataPulse. Simone Marchetti, CEO; Elena Varga, CTO. They're doing behavioral analytics for fashion e-commerce. So they track how customers browse, try to predict purchase intent, personalize recommendations. €450K ARR, four enterprise customers.

[15:40] Ava: €450K ARR is real traction.

[15:41] Tomás: It is. But, um, the space is really crowded. Segment, Amplitude, Mixpanel — they all do this. The differentiation is supposed to be, um, fashion-specific signals. Like how customers interact with fit and color.

[15:50] Daniel: Understood. But Mixpanel has those signals now too. What's the defensibility?

[15:52] Tomás: They claim they've got a proprietary model trained on, uh, 50 million fashion transactions. But that's not that defensible either.

[15:58] Ava: I think this is a pass. Good team, decent traction, but I don't see a moat. And the market is saturated.

[16:02] Daniel: Agreed. Pass on DataPulse. Last one?

[16:04] Tomás: UrbanFlow. Diana Rossi, CEO; Karim Haddad is COO. Micro-logistics using cargo e-bikes. So they do last-mile delivery for urban areas using electric cargo bikes instead of vans. They've got operations in, um, three Italian cities. 200 deliveries per week, €12K weekly revenue.

[16:13] Daniel: Wait, €12K per week? That's real revenue?

[16:14] Tomás: Yes. Forty-eight thousand per month, roughly. And growing.

[16:17] Ava: Unit economics?

[16:17] Tomás: Revenue per delivery is about €15. Cost per delivery, including rider salary, maintenance, all that, is about €8. So margins are, um, €7 per delivery. That's about 47% gross margin.

[16:27] Daniel: That's solid. And the expansion plan?

[16:29] Tomás: They want to hit six cities in the next 12 months. Each new city takes about three months to operationalize. Hiring and training riders, setting up drop-off points.

[16:36] Ava: What about sustainability of the unit economics as they scale? Do margins hold?

[16:40] Tomás: Diana's model shows they improve slightly because of fixed cost leverage. The bikes are cheaper after the first 500 deliveries per unit.

[16:47] Daniel: I like this. It's a real business with real revenue. The only concern is, um, is this venture-scale? Can this get to, like, €50M revenue in five years?

[16:55] Ava: The TAM is large enough. European last-mile logistics is, what, hundreds of billions. Even a small percentage is huge.

[16:59] Daniel: Right. I want to invest in UrbanFlow. Strong yes. But, um, we should also look at their patent filings. Do they have any IP around the bike design or route optimization?

[17:03] Tomás: I'll add that to my list. Should take a day or so to pull.

[17:05] Daniel: Perfect. So we've got NovaTech as a strong yes, UrbanFlow as a strong yes, MindBridge as conditional, and we're passing on GreenLoop and DataPulse. That leaves us with — best case, two solid investments plus one conditional. Which means we've got, what, €200K-€250K per deal roughly?

[17:16] Ava: Assuming MindBridge clears the conditional. Which is a big assumption.

[17:18] Daniel: Right. Tomás, on Demo Day, let's make sure we dig hard on the conditional stuff with MindBridge. Unit economics, product validation, compliance.

[17:24] Tomás: Will do.

[17:25] Daniel: And, um, one other thing. Ava, you wanted to revisit the follow-on reserve policy before Demo Day?

[17:29] Ava: Yeah, I did. If we invest in two companies and one of them is performing really well by next summer, I want to have a follow-on option. I don't want to get diluted out or miss the next round.

[17:38] Daniel: That's fair. But let's, um, let's deal with that after we see how outcomes land. We don't want to overcommit before we see how these teams execute.

[17:46] Ava: Fair enough.

[17:47] Daniel: Alright, I think we're in good shape. Tomás, your action items: reference check on Yara, pull UrbanFlow's patent filings. Anything else?

[17:53] Tomás: Nope. Oh — venue stuff. How are we organized on the night? Breaks between pitches?

[17:58] Daniel: Right. So we've got five pitches, seven minutes each, so 35 minutes of pitches plus, um, let's say 10 minutes of administrative stuff. We'll do a 15-minute break between the third and fourth pitch to let people stretch.

[18:07] Ava: Drinks before the pitches or after?

[18:08] Daniel: Both. We'll have champagne and light appetizers from 17:00. Pitches start at 17:30. Then after the last pitch, we'll open it up for networking.

[18:15] Tomás: Should we prep any talking points on the deals?

[18:16] Daniel: Good idea. Let's, um, let's do a quick scorecard calibration. What metrics are we going to use?

[18:22] Ava: Team, market, product, traction, unit economics.

[18:24] Daniel: Right. Each scored 1 to 5, where 5 is, um, exceptional. And we just add them up?

[18:28] Tomás: Or we could weight them differently depending on the stage?

[18:30] Ava: We could but that gets complicated. Let me propose: we score each one, total is 25 points, and above 18 is a strong yes. Below 12 is a pass.

[18:39] Daniel: And between 12 and 18?

[18:40] Ava: Conditional. Depends on the startup and the context.

[18:42] Daniel: Okay, let's try that. But, um, I don't want to be too rigid about it. These are early-stage companies. Some have traction, some have great teams but are pre-revenue. We need to calibrate the scoring based on stage.

[18:54] Ava: Of course. So maybe we have different scorecards for revenue-stage versus pre-revenue?

[18:58] Tomás: Or we just, um, we just see how it goes? Like, we know what good looks like. We score them and then we talk about it.

[19:02] Daniel: Yeah, let's not overthink this. We know the quality of the teams. We know the market dynamics. Let's score them on Demo Day and just be thoughtful about it.

[19:09] Ava: Fair.

[19:10] Daniel: Anything else we should cover?

[19:11] Tomás: I think we're good. I'll send you both the updated dossiers on each founder. And I'll get you the reference check and patent stuff by, um, by the evening before Demo Day.

[19:19] Daniel: Perfect. Alright. Last thing — I know this is early, but let's just keep in mind our reserve capacity. We've got €600K deployed, and we're probably deploying €350K to €450K on Demo Day. That leaves us headroom for follow-ons or, um, or companies that come in through our network.

[19:33] Ava: Noted. And if MindBridge converts and we want to lead their next round?

[19:36] Daniel: We'll talk about it. One step at a time.

[19:38] Ava: Right.

[19:39] Daniel: Okay, I think we're done. Let's reconvene the morning of Demo Day and just do a quick final walkthrough.

[19:44] Tomás: Sounds good.

[19:45] Daniel: And, um, nice work on the pipeline, Tomás. This is a good cohort.

[19:48] Tomás: Thanks. I'm excited about NovaTech and UrbanFlow especially.

[19:51] Ava: Me too. Alright, let's get back to it.

[19:52] [END]

title: Demo Day Pitch: NovaTech AI
text:
# Demo Day Pitch: NovaTech AI
**Date:** November 12, 2025
**Time:** 17:35–17:52 CET
**Location:** Palazzo delle Industrie, Milan
**Participants:** Marco Bianchi (NovaTech CEO), Yara Osman (NovaTech CTO), Daniel Reeves (IC), Ava Lindström (IC), Tomás Herrera (Analyst)

---

[17:35] Daniel: Alright, everyone. Welcome to Demo Day. I'm Daniel Reeves with Vertex Minds. This is our first pitch of the evening, and I'm really excited to introduce NovaTech AI. Marco, Yara, please come on up.

[17:37] [applause, some audience rustling as the founders take their seats]

[17:38] Marco: Thank you. Um, hi everyone. I'm Marco Bianchi, CEO of NovaTech. And this is Yara Osman, our CTO. So, we're here to talk about a problem that costs the European architecture and construction industry billions every single year. [clicks to first slide]

[17:45] Marco: Architectural firms spend on average 40 hours per construction permit. That's one full work week per permit. And that time is spent basically reading, interpreting, and copying regulatory documents. It's boring, it's error-prone, and it's incredibly expensive.

[17:57] Marco: So, what we built is an AI system that automates this process. [clicks to product demo slide] We've fine-tuned a large language model on 200,000 building regulation documents — everything from Italian building codes to EU compliance standards. And now when an architect uploads a project brief, our system automatically extracts the relevant requirements, flags compliance risks, and even generates draft permit documentation.

[18:12] Yara: [takes over] And to be clear, the accuracy is critical here. So we've validated the model on, um, on held-out test sets with F1 scores of 0.92 for requirement extraction and 0.85 for compliance flagging. Which is, you know, in production-quality territory.

[18:27] Marco: We're currently operating with 12 beta customers. Three of them are paying. And the response has been fantastic. One studio in Milan told us, quote, "This saved us basically two weeks per project." That's real money.

[18:38] Marco: The ask is €200,000 for 8% of the company. We'll use this to hire two more engineers, expand our document training set, and begin sales outreach in the German and French markets.

[18:47] Marco: And, um, that's the pitch. Questions?

[18:48] [brief pause, audience leans in]

[18:50] Ava: So, Marco, I want to dig into the TAM. You mentioned architectural firms. But let me ask — how many architects in Europe? How are you thinking about the market size?

[18:59] Marco: Right. So the way we look at it, there's about 150,000 architectural practices in Europe. Average revenue, maybe €800K to €1M per practice. And we estimated, um, based on permit timelines, that the regulatory documentation market — the total addressable value of that time — is about €3.4 billion annually.

[19:19] [Ava makes a note, nods slowly but doesn't look convinced]

[19:20] Ava: Okay. So €3.4B. That's — that's larger than the estimate we had on the intake form. [glances at Tomás]

[19:25] Marco: Well, um, I mean, that's when you factor in, you know, the broader construction consulting market. It's not just pure architecture. It's also engineers, um, permit consultants.

[19:33] Ava: Got it. So the 150,000 architects — how many of those would actually use your product?

[19:39] Marco: We think, um, realistically, in the first three years, maybe 5%. So 7,500 firms. At, um, let's say €1,000 per month per firm, that's €90M ARR in three years.

[19:50] Ava: [pauses] And you're assuming 5% penetration just based on what?

[19:54] Marco: Based on, um, comparable adoption curves for, like, design software. AutoCAD, Revit. Those took several years to hit mainstream adoption.

[20:01] Ava: Right, but those were solving different problems and, um, they didn't require, you know, changing existing workflows the way your product might. Anyway, um, let me ask about validation. Yara, you mentioned 0.92 F1 on held-out test sets. But have you done any external benchmarking? Like, third-party evaluation?

[20:18] Yara: We have, um, we haven't actually done external benchmarking yet. But the test sets we used are pretty comprehensive. They include documents from, like, six different European jurisdictions. So I think the — the generalization should be good.

[20:30] Ava: But you don't know until you test it on data you didn't train on.

[20:33] Yara: That's fair. Yeah. We could do that.

[20:35] Daniel: [interjecting] What about the beta customers? Are they seeing the 0.92 accuracy in production?

[20:39] Marco: Um, it's a bit different in production, honestly. Because, um, the documents are messier. They're scans. Some of them are in Italian, some are in mixed — like, mixed Italian and English. So we're seeing maybe, um, 80 to 85% in actual use.

[20:53] Daniel: And the customers are okay with that?

[20:55] Marco: They are, because it's still saving them time. The product flags issues that, um, human reviewers still need to check. But it's a 5x speed improvement.

[21:03] Tomás: Marco, question on unit economics. You mentioned three paying customers. What's the monthly fee? And what's your customer acquisition cost?

[21:10] Marco: Um, so the first three are paying, I think, around €800 a month. Which is the startup pricing. We're planning to move to, um, to a usage-based model as we get more volume. Each firm essentially gets a bulk seat license.

[21:22] Tomás: So €800 monthly. And your CAC to land each one?

[21:25] Marco: [pause] It's been, um, really low so far because they came through personal networks. I'd say probably €2,000 to €3,000 to acquire each new customer when we go to broad market.

[21:34] Tomás: And your retention? Do you have any churn data?

[21:37] Marco: One beta customer has been with us for six months and is renewing. The other two are newer. So we don't have, um, a lot of data yet. But the initial signals are good.

[21:46] Daniel: [pauses] One more thing — patents. You mentioned patent-pending status in your materials. Can you walk us through what you've filed?

[21:53] Marco: Yeah, so we've, um, we've filed something on the document parsing architecture. The approach to fine-tuning on regulatory documents is somewhat novel. [looks to Yara]

[22:01] Yara: So basically we've built a custom tokenizer and a prompt-chaining architecture that's, um, it's tuned to regulatory language. A lot of existing LLMs don't handle that well. And we think there's some IP there.

[22:11] Daniel: But the filing is still, um, pending?

[22:13] Marco: Yes, it's, um — [pause] — it's currently in draft. We're planning to file within the next month or so.

[22:19] Ava: Okay. So no formal patent application yet.

[22:21] Yara: That's correct.

[22:22] [brief silence, Ava and Daniel exchange a look]

[22:23] Daniel: Alright. I think we're good. You've got a real problem and a real solution. The team clearly understands the tech. A few things to tighten: external benchmarking, actual patent filing, longer cohort of retained customers. But this is exciting. [applause]

[22:37] Marco: Thank you so much.

[22:38] Ava: [nods] We'll be in touch with next steps.

[22:40] [audience applause, some murmuring. A few people in the audience lean over and whisper to neighbors. Energy in the room is visibly higher than before the pitch]

[22:45] Tomás: [to Daniel, quietly] That TAM number was different from the deck we reviewed.

[22:48] Daniel: [whispers back] Yeah. I caught that. We should flag it.

[22:50] [END]

title: Demo Day Pitch: GreenLoop
text:
# Demo Day Pitch: GreenLoop
**Date:** November 12, 2025
**Time:** 17:48–18:08 CET
**Location:** Palazzo delle Industrie, Milan
**Participants:** Luca Ferretti (GreenLoop CEO), Stefan Berger (GreenLoop COO), Daniel Reeves (IC), Ava Lindström (IC), Tomás Herrera (Analyst)

---

[17:48] Daniel: Alright, next up we have GreenLoop. Luca, Stefan, welcome.

[17:50] [scattered applause as founders take their seats]

[17:51] Luca: Thank you. Hi, I'm Luca Ferretti, CEO of GreenLoop. This is Stefan Berger, our COO. Um, so we're solving a really concrete problem in the construction industry. Every day, contractors finish projects and they've got leftover materials. Concrete, steel, wood, equipment. They need to get rid of it. And right now they're either, um, sending it to landfill or they're doing these inefficient, local handshake deals with other contractors.

[18:07] Luca: We built a B2B marketplace that connects contractors with surplus materials to contractors who need them. Think of it like a, um, like an eBay for construction excess.

[18:15] Luca: [clicks to traction slide] So far we've got 45 companies on the platform. We've done €180K in GMV over the last six months. That's, um, that's real transactions. And we're growing month-over-month.

[18:24] Luca: The way we make money is we take a 9% take rate on each transaction. So our revenue is, um, roughly €16K so far. The margins are good because the platform is basically a digital marketplace — low marginal cost once we've onboarded the sellers.

[18:36] Luca: [clicks to operational slide] What makes us special is our last-mile optimization. So it's not enough to match a buyer and seller. We also help coordinate pickup and delivery. Stefan's built a routing algorithm that, um, that minimizes pickup costs for both sides. It's a game-changer.

[18:48] Stefan: [nods] Yeah, so most transactions, we're saving 20% to 30% on logistics compared to standalone arrangements.

[18:54] Luca: Right. So we create value beyond just the match. Um, we're raising €150K for 6% of the company. We'll use this to expand from our current market, which is, um, primarily Northern Italy and Milan, to the broader EU. We think this model scales across Europe.

[19:08] Luca: And that's the pitch. Questions?

[19:09] [pause, few hands raise]

[19:10] Ava: Yeah, I've got some. So you mentioned 45 companies. How many active sellers versus buyers? And — more importantly — what's your customer acquisition cost?

[19:19] Luca: Good question. Um, so of the 45, maybe 30 are on the supply side. 15 on the demand side. And CAC is, um, [pause] it's been about €800 per seller. We've been doing in-person meetings, um, one-on-one pitches to construction firms.

[19:33] Ava: So €800 to acquire one seller. And on average, how much revenue does a seller generate?

[19:38] Luca: So the average transaction is, um, around €2,000. And we take 9%. So that's €180 per transaction.

[19:45] Ava: €180 per transaction. And each seller, on average, how many transactions do they do with you in a year?

[19:51] Luca: Um, probably two. Maybe two-and-a-half.

[19:54] Ava: So two transactions per year times €180 take rate is €360 in annual revenue per seller. And you paid €800 to acquire them.

[19:59] Luca: [defensive] But they have multiple buyers...

[20:01] Ava: [interrupting] Wait, let me finish. So you're spending €800 to earn €360 in the first year. When does that break even?

[20:07] Luca: Well, um, it's lifetime value. If they stay with us for three years, it's €1,080 in total take rate. So the payback is around two years.

[20:16] Ava: Two years is a long payback. And that assumes zero churn and no increase in CAC as you move into new markets. In Germany, for example, do you think CAC stays at €800?

[20:26] Luca: [pause, more defensive] It might be higher. But we're also working on, um, organic growth channels. Referrals, word-of-mouth. And we're building out our demand side so that sellers are getting more transaction opportunities.

[20:37] Ava: Okay. But you're not there yet. Right now, it's one-on-one outreach.

[20:41] Luca: Right, currently.

[20:42] Ava: Got it. Next question — your marketplace is geographically limited, right? Pickup and delivery logistics only work within a certain radius?

[20:49] Luca: Um, yeah. Our current model is optimized for, like, a 50km radius. Beyond that, logistics become prohibitive. Most of our transactions are within 25km.

[20:58] Ava: So you're regional, not national.

[21:00] Luca: We're regional now, but we're planning to, um, to build out regional hubs in other European cities.

[21:05] Ava: [skeptical] Regional hubs. So you'd need to replicate your operational model in each new market. That means local teams, local relationships, local pickup infrastructure.

[21:12] Luca: Yeah, that's the plan.

[21:14] Ava: Which is not like a software company. It's more like a, um, like a logistics network.

[21:18] Luca: [quietly] Well, yes and no. The software is highly leveraged. We build the routing algorithm once—

[21:22] Ava: [interrupting] I understand. But your capital intensity is going to be high. You're going to need a team in each market. That's not scalable in the way we usually think about software.

[21:30] [uncomfortable pause in the room, audience energy noticeably shifts]

[21:31] Daniel: Stefan, can I ask — what's your background? Where did you come from?

[21:35] Stefan: I was a COO at a logistics company for three years. Before that, supply chain management at a manufacturing firm.

[21:41] Daniel: And you're comfortable building out a multi-market logistics network?

[21:44] Stefan: I've done it before. The complexity is real, but it's doable.

[21:48] Daniel: [nods slowly but noncommittally] Interesting.

[21:49] Tomás: So I want to, um, I want to circle back to the CAC for a second. You said €800 per seller in Northern Italy. But when you expand to Germany, do you think you can do that organically or — or are you going to need even more in-person effort?

[21:60] Luca: It'll probably take more effort initially. Germany's a bigger market but also more fragmented. We might need to, um, hire local teams to do the intro meetings.

[22:06] Tomás: Right. So CAC could double.

[22:08] Luca: Potentially.

[22:09] Tomás: So your unit economics get worse before they get better.

[22:12] Luca: [pause] In the near term, yes. But we're betting that as we build supply on the demand side, um, referrals and word-of-mouth kick in. And we can shift to a more, um, a more scalable acquisition model.

[22:23] Ava: But you don't have evidence of that yet.

[22:25] Luca: Not yet.

[22:26] [another uncomfortable pause]

[22:27] Daniel: Alright. I think we've got the picture. [glances at Ava, who's looking at her notes] The problem is real. Construction waste is a real problem. But the unit economics and the geographic expansion challenge are significant. We'll need to, um, we'll need to think carefully about whether this is venture-scale.

[22:42] Luca: Sure. I understand.

[22:43] Ava: [looking up] One more quick thing — how much of your €180K GMV is recurring? Like, what's the percentage of repeat sellers?

[22:50] Luca: Um, about 60% of our sellers have done more than one transaction.

[22:54] Ava: 60%. That's decent. But new sellers are only doing 1.4 transactions on average?

[22:59] Luca: [defensive again] We're early. Retention will improve as we expand supply.

[23:02] Ava: Maybe. But right now, repeat transaction rate is low. That's a concern.

[23:07] Luca: Fair point.

[23:08] Daniel: Okay. Thanks for the pitch. We'll follow up. [polite applause]

[23:10] [applause is noticeably quieter than after NovaTech. A few people in the audience are checking their phones. The energy has definitely cooled]

[23:14] Tomás: [quietly to Daniel] I don't think we're investing in this one.

[23:16] Daniel: [whispers back] Yeah. The math doesn't work.

[23:18] [END]

title: Demo Day Pitch: MindBridge
text:
# Demo Day Pitch: MindBridge
**Date:** November 12, 2025
**Time:** 18:03–18:23 CET
**Location:** Palazzo delle Industrie, Milan
**Participants:** Anna Kowalski (MindBridge CEO), Raj Patel (MindBridge CTO), Daniel Reeves (IC), Ava Lindström (IC), Tomás Herrera (Analyst)

---

[18:03] Daniel: Alright, welcome back everyone. Short break and then we move forward with MindBridge. Anna, Raj, come on up.

[18:05] [scattered applause, some people still getting back to their seats with drinks]

[18:07] Anna: Thank you. Um, hi. I'm Anna Kowalski, CEO of MindBridge. This is Raj Patel, our CTO. [pause, Anna glances at her notes] So, um, the problem we're solving is actually a pretty expensive one for companies. Employee turnover costs are massive. When someone leaves, a company loses 50% to 200% of that person's annual salary in replacement costs. That's recruiting, onboarding, lost productivity, knowledge transfer. It's a big number.

[18:27] Anna: Most companies only realize someone's about to leave when they hand in their resignation. But what if we could predict it? Weeks or months in advance?

[18:35] Anna: [clicks to product slide] We built MindBridge. It's a platform that analyzes internal communications — email, Slack messages, meeting behavior — and it predicts employee engagement and flight risk. Our dashboard gives HR leaders real-time visibility into team health.

[18:47] Raj: [taking over] From the tech side, we're using a combination of NLP and graph analysis. So we tokenize sentiment from written communications, we build an interaction graph to identify isolation or lack of connection, and then we feed those signals into a predictive model. The model is trained on historical data from our pilot customers. And it's achieving, um, 72% accuracy on identifying employees at high risk of leaving within the next quarter.

[19:07] Anna: [taking back] We've got three enterprise pilots right now. They're with mid-sized companies in tech and financial services. No revenue yet, but the pilots are generating a lot of interest. We're seeing strong engagement with the dashboard.

[19:18] Anna: [clicks to funding slide] We're asking for €180K for 8%. We'll use this to accelerate the go-to-market process and, um, hopefully convert these pilots to paying customers.

[19:27] Anna: That's the pitch. Questions?

[19:28] [Ava and Daniel exchange a glance, then Ava raises her hand]

[19:30] Daniel: So, Raj, the 72% accuracy — is that on historical data or live predictions?

[19:35] Raj: That's on historical data. We took a dataset from, um, from a partner company, and we backtested the model. So we trained on, like, 18 months of communication and, um, labeled outcomes of who actually left. And the model gets 72% AUC on that task.

[19:49] Daniel: But you haven't deployed this in production with a live cohort?

[19:52] Raj: [pause] Not yet, no. The pilots are, um, the pilots are early. They're just now starting to use it. So we won't have ground truth for a few months.

[19:59] Daniel: So in other words, you don't actually know how accurate it is when it's making real predictions.

[20:04] Raj: [defensively] The backtesting is pretty rigorous. We use cross-validation, we separate train and test sets. So I'm fairly confident the results will hold.

[20:11] Ava: But you don't know. You're confident, but you don't know.

[20:14] Raj: [quiet] That's fair.

[20:15] Ava: Okay, so, Anna, bigger question. You're analyzing employee communications. That's GDPR territory. Walk me through how you're handling data privacy.

[20:24] Anna: Right, great question. So we've, um, we've built in a few safeguards. First, we're processing only anonymized communications. And second, we've implemented data minimization — so we're only retaining the signals we need for the model, not the actual text of messages.

[20:38] Ava: Anonymized how?

[20:40] Anna: [pause] Well, so, um, we're stripping email addresses and names from the communication metadata. And then we're just looking at the graph structure and the sentiment signals.

[20:50] Ava: But you can probably re-identify people pretty easily from the graph structure. If I know someone works at a certain company and I see an interaction graph, I can figure out who's who.

[20:59] Anna: [uncomfortable pause] That's... that's a fair point. We haven't actually tested for re-identification risk. But we're, um, we're working with a GDPR consultant on this.

[21:08] Ava: Who's the consultant?

[21:10] Anna: Um, I can give you the name after the pitch.

[21:12] Ava: [skeptical] Okay. So in other words, the GDPR strategy is not fully baked.

[21:16] Anna: We're still refining it, yes.

[21:18] Tomás: Can I ask about the pilots? You said three enterprise pilots. How close are they to converting to paid?

[21:24] Anna: Um, so we're... we're optimistic. We're running pilots through Q4, and we expect at least two of them to sign contracts by Q1.

[21:32] Tomás: Based on what? Like, have they given you verbal commitments? What's the basis for that expectation?

[21:38] Anna: [hesitant] So, um, we've had positive feedback from the pilot sponsors. They're using the tool regularly. Um, but I won't say we have, like, signed LOIs yet.

[21:47] Tomás: So it's optimism.

[21:48] Anna: [defensive] It's based on actual usage, but yes, it's still optimistic.

[21:52] Ava: What's the pricing model? How much would you charge if they convert?

[21:56] Anna: So we're thinking, um, a per-employee seat model. So for a company with 500 people, maybe €2,000 a month. That's, like, €4 per person per month.

[22:04] Ava: So a 500-person company pays €24K per year. What's your CAC? What does it cost you to close that deal?

[22:10] Anna: We're not sure yet, since we haven't actually closed one.

[22:12] Ava: [laughs, not unkindly] Right. So you're at the stage where you're making pricing assumptions based on comparable products, but you don't have any real data.

[22:20] Anna: [quietly] That's accurate.

[22:21] Daniel: [interjecting, slightly more gently] So, Anna, I think the problem is real. I know a lot of HR leaders who would pay for better visibility into team health. But, um, you're still pretty early. The model isn't validated in production. The GDPR story isn't finalized. And you don't have paying customers yet.

[22:37] Daniel: [continues] The good news is the team is solid. Raj clearly understands the tech. And you're getting good traction in the pilots. But you need to de-risk a few things before we're comfortable investing.

[22:48] Anna: [nods] I understand. Um, what would it take to move from, like, conditional interest to a yes?

[22:53] Ava: [jumping in] Get one pilot to convert. Get external validation on your model accuracy. And get a definitive GDPR sign-off from a qualified DPA or legal counsel.

[23:01] Anna: Okay, so those are the three things.

[23:02] Ava: Yep. If you can do those in the next, um, three to six months, we'd be very interested in revisiting the conversation.

[23:09] Anna: [nods] Thank you. That's really helpful.

[23:10] Daniel: [standing slightly] Good luck with the pilots. [handshake]

[23:12] [polite applause, quieter than NovaTech but warmer than GreenLoop. Some people in the audience are nodding — there's a sense of respect for the team but skepticism about the maturity]

[23:16] Tomás: [to Daniel, quietly] I'd put that as conditional, no higher.

[23:18] Daniel: [whispers back] Yeah. They need to prove something real first.

[23:21] [END]

title: Meeting Transcript: Demo Day Alpha - DataPulse Pitch
text:
# Meeting Transcript: Demo Day Alpha - DataPulse Pitch
**Date:** November 12, 2025
**Location:** Palazzo delle Industrie, Milan
**Event:** Demo Day Alpha - Fourth Pitch
**Attendees:** Daniel Reeves (CEO, Vertex Minds), Ava Lindström (Co-IC, Vertex Minds), Tomás Herrera (Analyst, Vertex Minds), Simone Marchetti (Founder/CEO, DataPulse), Elena Varga (CTO, DataPulse)

---

[18:28] **Simone Marchetti:** Thank you, thank you everyone. Okay, so, um, we're DataPulse. And if you... if you work in fashion e-commerce, you know this problem intimately. Your customers are bouncing. They're clicking around your site, adding things to carts, and then just... [pauses, gestures widely] ...leaving. Why? You don't know. You have Google Analytics, sure, but it tells you *what* happened, not *why*.

[Simone clicks to next slide — bright, minimalist design with a fashion model silhouette and behavioral arrows overlaid]

**Simone:** We give you the *why*. Our behavioral analytics platform ingests your customer journey — every click, every scroll, every pause — and uses machine learning to predict churn, optimize product placement, and honestly, just make your store feel less like a maze.

[18:32] **Daniel:** [nods, taking notes]

**Simone:** We've got 50,000 monthly active users across our client base right now. Four enterprise clients, ranging from luxury accessories to fast fashion. And, uh, our core metric is this one: [points to slide] we're delivering an average 18% improvement in conversion lift. One client went from 2.1% to 2.48% conversion rate in six weeks.

[Simone clicks through revenue slide]

**Simone:** Current MRR is eight thousand euros. We're growing, we're profitable at the unit level, and our churn — our monthly churn is, um, pretty healthy at six percent.

[Elena Varga nods from the side. Someone in the audience shifts in their seat.]

[18:36] **Ava:** Beautiful deck, Simone. Really well put together. [pauses, looks down at notes] So I want to dig into that 50,000 MAU number. When we accessed the analytics dashboard you shared for due diligence last week, we counted around 38,000 active accounts in October. Can you help us reconcile that discrepancy?

[Simone's expression flattens slightly. A beat of silence.]

**Simone:** Oh, um. Right. Yeah, so those numbers, they do fluctuate seasonally, actually. October was a dip for us — summer's usually when fashion retail peaks, and then we see a seasonal drop-off in early fall. So our typical range is... is above 45,000, but October we were closer to the high 30s.

[18:39] **Tomás:** Do you have the September and October cohort data handy? I'm curious about the trajectory.

**Simone:** I, uh... Elena might have the exact figures, but we can definitely pull that post-call. We track it monthly.

[Elena: "Yeah, we have it in our database. I can send a dashboard link."]

**Simone:** Right. So the 50K is not like a, a guaranteed floor, it's more the recent high we're tracking toward.

[18:41] **Ava:** [makes a note] Okay. I want to move to churn. You said six percent monthly. That's... that math works out to about 72% annual churn. That concerns me significantly for a platform business. What's driving that churn?

[Simone leans forward]

**Simone:** So, the thing is — and this is actually something I wish more investors understood — it's not uniform churn. Our enterprise clients, the ones paying 2K, 3K a month, they retain incredibly well. It's mostly the smaller merchants, the solo sellers, who churn out. They try us, they realize they need more hands-on support to really implement the behavioral insights, and they move on. It's, it's almost expected at that segment.

[18:44] **Ava:** Right, I hear you. But let me ask you this then: what percentage of your eight thousand MRR comes from your largest client?

[Simone hesitates. Goes to answer, stops.]

**Simone:** About, um, forty percent. Yeah, roughly forty.

[Someone's phone rings. Loud, distinct ringtone — classical piano piece. A man in the third row fumbles to silence it. Audible apologies.]

[18:46] **Daniel:** [clears throat, maintains focus] Go ahead, Simone.

**Simone:** Sorry, yeah, so, um, forty percent from our top client, but like I said, the enterprise base is really sticky. Our churn at that level is, I'd say, less than two percent monthly.

[Ava nods, writing. Tomás and Daniel exchange a glance.]

[18:48] **Ava:** So you've got concentration risk and then churn risk at the SMB level, which is the bulk of your user base by count. That's the tension I'm seeing.

**Simone:** I mean, yes, but we're actively working on product-market fit at that level. We just released a no-code template system so smaller merchants can, uh, basically configure behavioral flows without engineering support. We think that unlocks retention.

[Elena chimes in: "We have beta data from three pilots. Churn dropped to 4.2% in those cohorts."]

[18:50] **Daniel:** Elena, good. Can you walk us through your technical architecture? I'm curious how you're processing that behavioral data in real-time without creating latency issues for customer-facing pages.

[Elena stands, clicks a slide showing a system diagram]

**Elena:** Yeah, so, um, we don't actually process in real-time for attribution. That would kill performance. Instead, we ingest events asynchronously via a lightweight JavaScript SDK — it's about 12 kilobytes, minified — and batch those into our data pipeline. The latency for dashboard updates is, um, usually within two to four hours. For real-time stuff like churn prediction alerts, we run a separate inference engine that samples data every... [pauses, checking notes on her laptop] ...every thirty minutes.

**Daniel:** And you own the data pipeline end-to-end?

**Elena:** Yep. We're not using third-party analytics vendors. It's all custom-built on top of a TimescaleDB instance, and then we have Python services running the ML models.

[Daniel nods, satisfied]

[18:54] **Tomás:** Simone, I want to ask about competitive moat. I know there's a few other behavioral analytics tools in the e-commerce space now. What's protecting DataPulse from commoditization?

[Simone sits back, more confident now]

**Simone:** Great question. So a lot of what we do is built on proprietary behavioral taxonomy that we've developed over eighteen months. We've categorized, I don't know, maybe five thousand distinct behavior patterns in fashion retail specifically. Our models were trained on that domain-specific data, so they perform way better for apparel merchants than a generic tool like, say, something trained on SaaS or marketplace data.

[Simone clicks to a slide showing accuracy metrics]

**Simone:** We published some of this research at, um, an e-commerce conference last spring. And because of that taxonomy and the domain training, we can make predictions that generic competitors just can't. For instance, we can flag when a customer is likely to buy in a higher price tier, not just whether they'll churn. That's the moat.

[Audience murmurs, a few nods]

[19:00] **Ava:** How long did it take to build that taxonomy?

**Simone:** About sixteen months. We worked with, um, fashion merchants directly, coded up patterns, tested, iterated. It's not something you can, uh, easily replicate if you're a generic analytics company trying to add fashion as a vertical.

[19:02] **Tomás:** And you've kept that proprietary? You haven't open-sourced it?

**Simone:** No, no, it's our core IP. We have a patent pending on the classification methodology, actually. [Elena nods in confirmation] So I think we're well-protected there.

[Daniel taps his pen against his notes]

[19:04] **Daniel:** One last thing from me — you're currently at eight thousand MRR, and you said you're profitable. Where are you putting your focus for the next six months? More customer acquisition, or product development?

**Simone:** Product development, mainly. The no-code template thing Elena mentioned, that's phase one. Phase two is expanding our model coverage — so right now we focus on conversion and churn prediction, but we want to add size recommendation models, return rate prediction, um, fashion trend detection for inventory. We think that gets us closer to being an indispensable platform rather than just an analytics tool.

[19:07] **Daniel:** Okay. And funding needs?

**Simone:** We haven't formally closed a round yet, but yes, we're looking to raise. We'd be talking to investors soon after the new year, probably, to fund the product roadmap and, um, a dedicated sales person.

[Ava raises her eyebrows slightly, makes a note]

[19:08] **Daniel:** Thanks, Simone. [pauses] Elena, thanks for the technical context.

**Simone:** Thank you. [stands, shakes hands with Daniel and Ava. Elena does the same.] Really appreciate the questions.

[Simone and Elena gather their materials. Tomás leans in to Ava and Daniel]

[19:10] **Tomás:** [quietly] The 50K MAU gap is going to be a problem in diligence. And that top-ten-client concentration...

**Ava:** [quietly] Yeah. Product's solid, though. Simone's a good operator. But the metrics don't quite match the narrative.

**Daniel:** [quietly] Let's see if they want to do a follow-up. I want deeper churn data before we move forward.

[The three make notes. An event coordinator signals that the next pitch will begin in five minutes. The audience applauds as Simone and Elena exit the stage.]

[19:12] **[END OF TRANSCRIPT]**

---

## Summary Notes
- **Strength:** Domain-specific behavioral taxonomy, strong CTO, compelling product-market fit narrative
- **Concerns:** MAU verification gap (38K vs. claimed 50K), high SMB churn (6% monthly), revenue concentration (40% from one client)
- **Follow-up Actions:** Request September–October cohort data, churn breakdown by customer segment, patent filing details, product roadmap timeline
- **Investor Sentiment:** Mixed to cautiously interested; diligence required before term sheet

title: Meeting Transcript: Demo Day Alpha - UrbanFlow Pitch
text:
# Meeting Transcript: Demo Day Alpha - UrbanFlow Pitch
**Date:** November 12, 2025
**Location:** Palazzo delle Industrie, Milan
**Event:** Demo Day Alpha - Fifth and Final Pitch
**Attendees:** Daniel Reeves (CEO, Vertex Minds), Ava Lindström (Co-IC, Vertex Minds), Tomás Herrera (Analyst, Vertex Minds), Diana Rossi (Founder/CEO, UrbanFlow), Karim Haddad (Operations Lead, UrbanFlow)

---

[18:45] **Diana Rossi:** [walks to center of stage with energy, smiles at the crowd] Okay, everyone, last pitch of the night. I promise to make it worth your time.

[Audience chuckles. Diana clicks to her first slide — bold typography with a photo of an electric cargo e-bike loaded with packages, Milan's Duomo in the background]

**Diana:** This is the last-mile delivery problem. You run an e-commerce shop, you're selling, I don't know, coffee, shoes, candles — something people buy online. You've got maybe 50 to 500 orders a day depending on your size. You ship them using standard couriers, and your customer gets their package in... three to five days. Or they don't get it at all, because it gets lost in a warehouse outside the city.

[Diana clicks to next slide: a photo of a congested Milan street with cars, buses, and delivery vans]

**Diana:** Meanwhile, you're spending fifteen to twenty percent of your revenue on last-mile logistics. The courier margins suck. The customer experience is whatever. And the environment is... [pauses] ...not great.

[18:50] **Diana:** So we built UrbanFlow. We started in Bologna in August. We have one hub — Karim runs it, actually. [Karim waves from the side of the stage] We're doing about 2,100 deliveries a month with thirty-five merchant partners. We're charging them two-fifty per package, which is about forty percent cheaper than traditional courier rates. And we're making money.

[Diana clicks to financial slide]

**Diana:** We're at six thousand euros MRR. Gross margin is twenty-eight percent. And honestly, we're just getting started.

[The audience shifts forward slightly. People scribbling notes]

[18:54] **Ava:** [immediately] Beautiful pitch, Diana. Really compelling problem statement. Let's dig into the unit economics because that's where logistics lives or dies. Two-fifty revenue per delivery — walk me through your cost structure.

[Diana nods, clicks to a detailed cost breakdown slide]
